title: Nuno-felted scarf on silk habotai
subtitle: Merino roving felted into a silk base to produce a gathered, crinkled surface
excerpt: Nuno felting bonds merino fibres into an open-weave silk or silk-blend fabric through agitation. As the wool fibres migrate through the weave and lock, they gather the silk into a crinkled, textured surface. The finished nuno scarf is lighter and drapier than a fully felted piece and has a distinctive bubbled texture where the wool gathered the silk. This guide uses silk habotai, the most reliable base cloth for beginners.
sourceNotes: Nuno felting was developed and documented by Polly Stirling in the 1990s, building on traditional Central Asian felting on fibre-reinforced cloth. The technique is now widely taught. This guide uses standard workshop method rather than Stirling's own published text (which remains in copyright).

content.text: ) felting bonds wool into silk through

: wet agitation drives wool fibres through the silk weave and locks them on the reverse. As the wool shrinks, it gathers the silk into texture. Silk habotai (8 mm weight) works reliably for beginners because its open plain weave allows fibres to migrate without excessive force.

items.name: Silk habotai, 8 mm weight, approximately 160 x 45 cm
Hot water and cold water access
Bamboo rolling mat (longer than the silk)

content.text: Step 1: Lay roving on the silk

Place the silk on the bamboo mat. Lay thin wisps of merino across the silk in one direction, covering perhaps 60-70% of the surface (partial coverage produces more texture than full coverage). A second very sparse layer at 45 degrees adds variation but is optional. The roving coverage determines how much the scarf gathers: heavy coverage produces a very textured, shorter piece; light coverage produces a longer, less gathered piece.

Place the felting net over the work. Pour warm (not hot) soapy water gently over the net. Use warm rather than hot water for silk: very hot water can shift the silk or distort the weave before the fibres have locked. Press down through the net with flat palms until fully saturated.

Rub through the net in circular strokes for 10 minutes. When the roving no longer shifts under the net, remove the net and rub directly, using more pressure. Test for migration by lifting the edge of the wool away from the silk: at the start, it peels off cleanly; once migration begins, you will feel resistance and then tearing if you pull hard. Stop pulling; just check.

Once migration has begun (fibres resist peeling), roll the piece tightly in the mat and roll 50-80 passes. The silk will begin to gather as you roll; this is the expected result. Unroll, check, and roll from the other end. Continue until the piece has gathered by 20-30% in width and the silk surface is evenly textured.

Rinse in cool water. Do not wring; press gently in a towel to remove excess water. Smooth out the scarf to your preferred shape and hang to dry, or dry flat if you want the texture to set in a particular direction.

items.fix: Re-wet with warm soapy water and rub more vigorously before rolling. The roving must resist peeling before rolling begins.
items.cause: Migration did not fully occur before rolling stopped
items.problem: Roving peels off the silk after drying

items.fix: Lay the roving in uneven wisps, not solid bands. Uneven coverage gives a random bubble texture. Banded coverage gives ridges that look corrugated.
items.cause: Heavy roving coverage in bands that gathered the silk into folds rather than even texture
items.problem: The silk crinkles into hard ridges rather than soft bubbles

items.fix: Use warm rather than hot water for silk nuno. Check the piece frequently during rolling and stop when the texture is achieved, before full merino shrinkage occurs.
items.cause: Hot water drove fast migration and heavy fulling in the first rolling session
items.problem: The scarf shrank much more than expected
